question: Why did India stay poor while the rest of Asia got rich?

theme.label: Economy and public money
readerQuestions: What is the headline economic number?
Is this a total number or a per-person number?
How much of the story is prices, exchange rates, or GDP share?
What does this explain about household life?

plannedCharts.title: The same starting line, and the great divergence
plannedCharts.subtitle: Maddison Project 2023 · GDP per capita in 2011 int-$ · a long-run reconstruction, read as broad trajectory not precise levels
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Around 1950 India was no poorer than its Asian peers; this is the gap that opened up afterward.

plannedCharts.title: The income gap, measured
plannedCharts.unit: GDP per capita at PPP (constant 2021 int-$)
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · GDP per capita at purchasing-power parity, constant 2021 international dollars
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The measured, modern version of the gap, on a like-for-like PPP basis.

plannedCharts.title: How long people live
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · life expectancy at birth · 1960 to latest
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: East Asia invested in survival first; the life-expectancy gap opened decades before the income gap.

plannedCharts.title: Children who don't reach five
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · under-five mortality · 1960 to latest
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: A healthy, surviving child is the first input into a future workforce.

plannedCharts.title: Years of schooling, the long view
plannedCharts.subtitle: Lee-Lee / Barro-Lee via Our World in Data · average years of schooling, adults · the human-capital stock, not just enrolment
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Schooling is the human-capital stock a country brings to industrialisation; India started this climb late and slow.

plannedCharts.title: Where are the women at work?
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank / ILO modelled estimate · share of women 15+ in the labour force
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: India's single most striking outlier: barely a quarter to a third of women are in the measured workforce, far below its Asian peers.

plannedCharts.title: How fast families shrank
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · total fertility rate · 1960 to latest
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Faster fertility decline opened the demographic-dividend window earlier in East Asia.

plannedCharts.title: Children too short for their age
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · child stunting, latest available year per country
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Stunting is malnutrition written into a child's body and brain; India's rate is high even against much poorer countries.

plannedCharts.title: How much each country built
plannedCharts.unit: gross fixed capital formation (% of GDP)
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · gross fixed capital formation as a share of GDP · the investment rate
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The under-told engine of the miracle: East Asia poured a huge share of output into factories, roads and machines.

plannedCharts.title: Who saved to pay for it
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · gross savings as a share of GDP
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: High investment was financed by high domestic saving; the two move together.

plannedCharts.title: Foreign money coming in
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · foreign direct investment, net inflows, as a share of GDP
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Foreign factories brought capital, technology and export markets; Vietnam and China pulled in far more, relative to their size.

plannedCharts.title: The factory escalator India never rode
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · manufacturing value added as a share of GDP · India and South Korea have full records; China's WB series starts only in 2004
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Manufacturing is the escalator out of poverty; Korea's share humped up and stayed, India's never really rose.

plannedCharts.title: India's leap from farm to office
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · India's value added by sector · the structural leap that skipped the factory
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: India's workers and output moved from farms to services, largely skipping the mass-manufacturing stage in between.

plannedCharts.title: Who left the farm, and when
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank / ILO modelled estimate · share of workers in agriculture
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Development means moving workers off the farm into higher-productivity work; India did this slower than its peers.

plannedCharts.title: Climbing the complexity ladder
plannedCharts.subtitle: Harvard Growth Lab Atlas of Economic Complexity · how diverse and sophisticated each country's exports are
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: It is not just how much you export but what: Korea and China climbed to complex, high-value goods while India's basket stayed simpler.

plannedCharts.title: Making things to sell the world
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · manufactured goods as a share of merchandise exports
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Export-led growth runs on selling manufactures to the world; the peers built that engine, India leaned more on services and commodities.

plannedCharts.title: How much each worker produces
plannedCharts.subtitle: Penn World Table via Our World in Data · real GDP per hour worked
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The income gap is, at bottom, a productivity gap: how much value an hour of work creates.

plannedCharts.title: Spending on inventing the future
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · gross domestic expenditure on research and development as a share of GDP
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Korea now spends a world-leading share on R&D; India's has been stuck below one percent for decades.

plannedCharts.title: The power to run a factory
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · electric power consumption per person · a proxy for industrial and household capacity
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Power per person tracks how industrial and how comfortable a society has become.

plannedCharts.title: Moving to the city
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · share of population living in urban areas
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Factories and services cluster in cities; East Asia urbanised fast, India more slowly and messily.

plannedCharts.title: The poverty India did crush
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank Poverty and Inequality Platform via Our World in Data · share below the $3-a-day extreme-poverty line
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: India's genuine win: hundreds of millions lifted out of extreme poverty, even without the manufacturing boom.

plannedCharts.title: The all-in human scorecard
plannedCharts.subtitle: UNDP via Our World in Data · Human Development Index, combining health, schooling and income
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: A single composite of health, schooling and income, to see the whole gap at once.

plannedCharts.title: The road not taken: democracy
plannedCharts.subtitle: V-Dem via Our World in Data · electoral democracy index · India stayed democratic; Korea and Taiwan democratised only after their growth takeoff
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The honest counterweight: India built its record under continuous democracy, while the East Asian transformations were often coerced and only democratised later.

selectionRules: Start from the page question, not from the indicator name.
Choose the smallest set of visuals that explains scale, trend, structure, comparison, and caveat.
Prefer mobile-friendly charts: line charts with sparse ticks, horizontal bars, share strips, pyramids only when essential, and no dense tables.
Use one strong pull quote only when the evidence supports it with a number.
Add glossary blocks for technical terms that normal readers may not know.
caveats: Use only figures present in lockedNumbers or sourceSummaries.
If a related theme indicator is present, you may use it for context, but do not pretend it is the main answer.
If source coverage is thin, say so directly.
Do not compare states, districts, or cities unless the evidence packet contains those geographies.
forbiddenClaims: Do not infer causality from a trend unless the evidence explicitly supports it.
Do not cite a source that is not present in sourceSummaries.
